picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.

picks.name: Amazon.com Inc.

picks.entryNote: Three top-tier institutional allocators have added or initiated in Q1 2026 at prices near or above current levels; today's risk-off tape (S&P -1.6% midday) provides an intraday improvement in entry quality; AWS operating margins have been expanding structurally — the market has not fully priced the long-term margin normalization

picks.name: Oracle Corporation
picks.rationale: Oracle reported record Q4 FY2026 results today with $638B in Remaining Performance Obligations (+$85B in the quarter alone), cloud revenue growing 47% YoY to $9.91B, and guided Q1 FY2027 for 27-29% revenue growth vs. the 25% analyst estimate. picks.name: Exxon Mobil Corporation

Today's May CPI confirmed energy drove 60%+ of monthly price gains with a 23.5% 12-month energy price surge — a direct revenue tailwind for XOM. Iran war escalation fears remain acute (Trump warned today Iran 'will pay the price'), keeping the geopolitical risk premium structural through at least H2 2026. Morgan Stanley's base case of crude ~$90/barrel by year-end supports XOM's earnings floor.

picks.entryNote: Energy stocks up 22%+ YTD but still trading at ~32% discount to own 10-year P/E average — rare value-plus-momentum combination; XOM's dividend yield (~3.5%) provides income while waiting for further geopolitical risk premium expansion; Morningstar notes energy leaders are not undervalued by traditional metrics, so size accordingly
picks.exitTrigger: Ceasefire in Iran conflict + Strait of Hormuz reopening causing crude to collapse below $70/barrel, eliminating the geopolitical risk premium

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: RTX is the premier defense-aerospace compounder benefiting from the structural defense spending supercycle ignited by the Iran war, NATO rebuilding commitments, and elevated geopolitical risk premia. Industrials have delivered 24.19% total return in the past year, and defense is the highest-conviction sub-sector within Industrials given multi-year government contract visibility and backlog. Elliott Management has been running activist campaigns in the industrials/consumer discretionary space, reflecting institutional conviction in the real-economy rotation. RTX's Pratt & Whitney and Collins Aerospace divisions provide both defense and commercial aerospace exposure — a dual tailwind as air travel demand recovers even in a strained consumer environment.
picks.catalyst: US defense supplemental spending bill / NATO procurement contracts for Stinger, Patriot systems amid Middle East escalation
picks.entryNote: RTX has been a top-6 holding continuously for the past 7 trading days in this book — thesis fully intact; forward P/E ~20x on growing defense EPS with backlog visibility; electrification/defense dual-use exposure differentiates from pure-play defense peers
picks.exitTrigger: Material reduction in US defense budget in FY2027 appropriations, or Pratt & Whitney GTF engine program faces new structural airworthiness grounding orders
picks.keyRisk: Supply chain execution risk for Pratt & Whitney GTF engine program remains an ongoing operational overhang that has pressured margins in recent quarters

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: Goldman Sachs is the highest-conviction name within Financials — the sector rated Outperform by multiple analysts for 2026 — given its dominant capital markets and advisory franchise positioned to benefit from the M&A/IPO pipeline revival and private credit boom. With the yield curve positively sloped at +38 bps (2s10s) and IG spreads tight at ~80 bps, the credit environment is supportive for deal flow. GS's investment banking revenue is highly levered to the exact conditions present: a nominal-GDP-growth environment, re-opening M&A pipelines post-trade-war clarity, and an AI capex financing boom requiring structured credit and equity capital markets services.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 investment banking revenue report (mid-July) — M&A advisory and underwriting fees expected to show meaningful YoY acceleration
picks.entryNote: Financials trade at a discount to the index on forward P/E (~14-15x) — value tier within a growth book; yield curve steepening supports NIM at GS's banking unit while capital markets is a pure-volume play; has been a top-6 position in this book for 7 consecutive trading days
picks.exitTrigger: Credit spreads widen sharply (HY OAS > 450 bps), deal activity stalls, or Fed hike cycle causes M&A backlog to freeze due to financing cost concerns
picks.keyRisk: If the Fed hikes rates in H2 2026, equity capital markets and M&A deal flow could seize up, directly impressing GS's highest-margin revenue lines

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: S&P Global is the highest-quality, most durable business model in the financial sector — a near-monopoly on credit ratings, financial data, and indices that generates recurring, high-margin revenue regardless of market direction. In a late-cycle environment where bond issuance remains robust (elevated Treasury supply, corporate refinancing needs), SPGI's ratings franchise is directly levered to debt capital markets volume. The AI data infrastructure buildout is also creating a new growth vector — financial data analytics and alternative data products — that the market has not fully re-rated. SPGI has been a continuous holding in this book for 7 trading days with thesis fully intact.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) showing debt issuance volume acceleration + AI-enhanced data product revenue contribution
picks.entryNote: SPGI has been in the portfolio continuously since June 1; forward P/E ~28x — premium to market but below the 5-year median for this quality tier; Morningstar maintains wide-moat rating; no single event concentration risk unlike pure-play tech
picks.exitTrigger: SEC or DOJ moves to break up the SPGI-Moody's ratings duopoly through regulatory action, or debt issuance collapses in a credit freeze
picks.keyRisk: Regulatory risk to the ratings oligopoly structure; if debt issuance volumes contract sharply in a credit event, ratings revenue falls directly

picks.name: Merck & Co. Inc.
picks.rationale: Healthcare is 'coiling toward a breakout' per the sector scorecard with XBI posting a 62.63% 1-year total return and $106B in biotech M&A in 2026 alone. MRK is the highest-quality, lowest-risk expression of pharma value within the book: Keytruda is the world's best-selling oncology drug, the pipeline is robust (ADC platform, pneumococcal vaccine Capvaxive), and the stock trades at a discount to the S&P 500 on forward P/E despite superior earnings quality. Healthcare's low correlation to tech volatility and energy prices provides genuine diversification within the growth book. The sector's below-10-year-median P/E (XLV at 19.94x) makes this an undervalued defensive growth holding.

picks.entryNote: MRK has traded in the book since June 1 (medium conviction); pharma sector faces patent cliff concerns on Keytruda post-2028 but this is a known, multi-year dynamic; current valuation does not adequately compensate for the pipeline optionality; XLV forward P/E 19.94x is below 10-year median
picks.exitTrigger: Keytruda patent cliff accelerates (earlier-than-expected biosimilar entry) or drug pricing legislation materially cuts oncology reimbursement rates
picks.keyRisk: Keytruda revenue concentration — the drug represents ~45% of total MRK revenue; any approval setback, biosimilar threat, or pricing action would disproportionately impair earnings

picks.name: SPDR Gold Trust
picks.rationale: Gold is the premier tail-risk hedge against the exact macro regime we are navigating: 4.2% headline CPI (confirmed today), Iran war oil shock, a barely-positive real policy rate of +45 bps, and growing US fiscal credibility concerns driving bond vigilante behavior. GLD has returned +28.4% over the past year vs. SPY's +25.9% while carrying a beta of just 0.51 and near-zero 0.07 correlation to SPY. Today's hot-but-in-line CPI print does not remove the structural case — energy-driven inflation with core at 2.9% signals the Fed remains trapped between hiking (crushing growth) and holding (inflaming energy pass-through), the exact environment where gold outperforms.
picks.catalyst: BOJ rate hike June 16 triggering yen carry-trade unwinding and a flight-to-safety bid for gold; any further Iran escalation tightening oil supply
picks.entryNote: GLD trading at ~$395-400 range; near-term ~5% pullback from recent highs provides a tactically better entry; the structural bull case (fiscal dominance, negative real rates, geopolitical premium) remains fully intact and has been a core defensive anchor since June 1
picks.exitTrigger: Credible Iran ceasefire + Fed successfully pivots to cutting cycle + US fiscal consolidation signals reduce safe-haven premium
picks.keyRisk: A rapid Iran peace deal coinciding with a Fed pivot to cuts could simultaneously compress the geopolitical risk premium and the inflation-hedge premium

picks.name: Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF

With headline CPI confirmed at 4.2% today and the savings rate at its lowest since mid-2022 (2.6%), income-generating equities with pricing power are structural beneficiaries. The fund's ~3.5% yield and 0.06% expense ratio make it the most cost-efficient income stream in the defensive sleeve, complementing GLD (no yield) and IEF (rate-sensitive fixed income).
picks.catalyst: Risk-off rotation into dividend quality accelerating post-CPI print and Iran escalation; June 17 FOMC hold confirmation reduces rate-hike-driven dividend stock selloff risk
picks.entryNote: SCHD has been the anchor defensive holding since June 1; the combination of 3.5% yield plus expected dividend growth of 8-10% per year creates a total return profile that beats cash on a 2-3 year horizon even without price appreciation; value tilt provides natural cushion in a risk-off tape
picks.exitTrigger: Recession-driven dividend cuts from major holdings or a sharp rotation back to pure-growth that makes the yield premium irrelevant
picks.keyRisk: Value/dividend factor underperforms if the AI growth bull market resumes with full force, creating opportunity cost drag vs. the growth sleeve

picks.entryNote: BRK.B has been the most consistent defensive anchor in this book since June 1; no single event concentration, no analyst coverage dependency, no AI hype multiple; in today's -1.6% S&P tape, BRK.B typically outperforms on a relative basis due to its cash drag acting as a natural buffer

picks.name: iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF
picks.rationale: Today's May CPI print (core +0.2% m/m, below the +0.3% estimate) is the first clear signal that core inflation may be peaking rather than re-accelerating — core commodities fell 0.1% and transportation services fell 0.6%, suggesting energy pass-through to core is limited. This gives the Fed cover to hold at the June 17 meeting and potentially begin cutting in H2 2026 if core stays contained, which would be directly bullish for intermediate duration Treasuries. IEF at 4.29% (5-year yield) and 4.55% (10-year yield) offers a meaningful yield cushion with convexity upside if rates decline. The 7-10 year duration sweet spot avoids the 30-year's fiscal-deficit term premium risk.
picks.catalyst: June 17 FOMC hold confirmation + Fed signals data-dependency rather than hike bias, driving front-end Treasury rally and IEF price appreciation
picks.entryNote: IEF yields are at multi-year highs, providing a better entry than at any point in 2023-2024; today's benign core CPI print is the first incremental bullish signal for duration since April; position serves as the true rate-sensitive hedge within the defensive sleeve
picks.exitTrigger: Core CPI re-accelerates above 0.4% m/m for two consecutive months, forcing Fed to hike 25-50 bps and driving 10-year yield above 5.25%
picks.keyRisk: BOJ hike on June 16 triggers yen carry-trade unwinding, forcing Japanese institutional investors to repatriate capital by selling US Treasuries — a near-term technical supply shock that could push yields sharply higher before fundamentals reassert

picks.name: The Coca-Cola Company
picks.rationale: Consumer Staples (XLP) is explicitly upgraded in the June 9-10 sector rotation scorecard — moving 'from Improving toward leadership as a defensive hedge' — consistent with the strained consumer backdrop confirmed by today's 4.2% headline CPI print and the personal saving rate at 2.6%. KO is the highest-quality, most globally diversified consumer staples compounder with unmatched brand pricing power in an inflationary environment, 62+ consecutive years of dividend growth, and a business model with no meaningful AI or geopolitical supply chain risk. Berkshire's continued 11.56% portfolio weighting in KO (top 3 holding) is the definitive institutional endorsement of the staples thesis.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings showing pricing power holding vs. volume trade-down; defensive rotation acceleration as CPI at 4.2% maintains consumer squeeze
picks.entryNote: XLP moving toward sector leadership per the June 10 scorecard update; KO forward P/E ~22x — slight premium to 10-year median but justified by pricing power moat and dividend growth; in a risk-off tape like today's (-1.6% S&P), consumer staples outperform systematically; has been a continuous holding since June 1

picks.keyRisk: Consumer trade-down accelerating faster than KO's pricing ability; at 4.2% headline CPI, lower-income consumers are already materially squeezed and KO's premium pricing could face elasticity limits

summary: The May CPI print (4.2% headline, core +0.2% m/m — below the +0.3% estimate) confirmed today as this portfolio was finalized: the inflation regime is driven overwhelmingly by energy (60%+ of monthly gains) with core showing tentative moderation, reducing the worst-case rate-hike scenario while leaving the Fed firmly on hold at June 17. The portfolio is positioned for a late-cycle stagflationary-adjacent regime: the growth book anchors in AI infrastructure (NVDA, AVGO, GOOGL as the monetization layer, AMZN as AWS infrastructure) with disciplined diversification into energy (XOM — cheapest sector at 32% discount to own 10-year P/E), defense-industrials (RTX — Iran war defense supercycle), financials/capital markets (GS, SPGI), and pharma value (MRK) — collectively spanning 6 distinct GICS sectors. All seven names carried over from June 9 (NVDA, GOOGL, AVGO, AMZN, ORCL, GS, RTX, SPGI, MRK, XOM) retain fully intact theses; ORCL was elevated from position 5 to a confirmed high-conviction add following today's record Q4 FY2026 results ($638B RPO backlog, cloud +47%). The dominant risk being underwritten is a BOJ rate hike on June 16 triggering yen carry-trade unwinding that generates a cross-asset shock for both US equities and Treasuries simultaneously — the one scenario that could pressure both sleeves of the book.
diversificationNote: The growth book spans 6 GICS sectors: Information Technology (NVDA, AVGO, ORCL — 3 names, at the hard cap), Communication Services (GOOGL), Consumer Discretionary (AMZN), Energy (XOM), Industrials (RTX), and Financials (GS, SPGI) plus Health Care (MRK); the two IT-semis names (NVDA, AVGO) share the AI capex secular theme but occupy different points in the value chain (GPU compute monopoly vs. custom-ASIC/networking), while the defensive sleeve spans four distinct exposures: gold/inflation hedge (GLD), broad dividend-quality equity (SCHD), all-weather conglomerate cash optionality (BRK.B), intermediate Treasury duration (IEF), and consumer staples pricing-power compounder (KO).

phaseData.macro:
Here is your senior macro strategy briefing for **Friday, June 5, 2026**:

---

## 📊 MACRO REGIME DASHBOARD — US EQUITIES | June 5, 2026

---

### THE DATA TAPE

**FED POLICY**

The Federal Reserve maintained the federal funds rate at 3.50%–3.75%
, now 
the third consecutive meeting where the committee chose to stand pat – following three consecutive cuts last year.
 The April 29 meeting was unusually contentious: 
the FOMC split along 8–4 lines, with officials expressing different reasons for their vote
 — 
the last time four members dissented was October 1992.
 On the dot plot, 
the median of the March 2026 Fed dot plot projections sees the fed funds rate falling just 25 bps in 2026 and 25 bps in 2027,
 implying a terminal rate around 3.25%. 
Notably, the "longer run" anchor has shifted upward to 3.0%, signaling a collective belief that the neutral rate is higher than previously estimated.
 
Fed officials foresee the funds rate settling down to its expected neutral level around 3.1%.
 Market-implied path: 
markets are pricing in no changes the rest of this year and well into 2027,
 with 
an ~85% chance of a quarter-point rate *hike* by year-end, up from 60% a week ago,
 as energy-driven inflation reaccelerates. The real policy rate (Fed funds midpoint 3.625% vs. core PCE ~3.3%) is barely positive — policy is only modestly restrictive relative to neutral at ~3.0–3.1%.

**YIELD CURVE**

As of June 4, 2026, the US Treasury yield curve is upward-sloping: the 2-Year yields 4.05%, the 10-Year 4.48%, and the 30-Year 4.98%.
 
The 10Y vs. 2Y spread (2s10s) is +42 bps; the 10Y vs. 3M spread (3m10y) is +76 bps.
 Crucially, 
the 10-year yield hovered around 4.48% after climbing on stronger-than-expected labor market data, reinforcing expectations of tighter Fed policy.
 
Elevated term premium and steady Treasury issuance continue to support higher long-maturity yields, consistent with upward pressure on long-end benchmarks.
 Term premium is rising — structurally driven by persistent inflation uncertainty and heavy sovereign supply.

**INFLATION — RE-ACCELERATING**

The annual inflation rate accelerated to 3.8% in April 2026, the highest since May 2023, compared to 3.3% in March — above forecasts of 3.7% — as the oil shock triggered by the war with Iran continues to push prices higher.
 
Energy costs jumped 17.9%, the steepest annual increase since September 2022, with gasoline up 28.4% and fuel oil up 54.3%.
 
Core CPI edged higher to 2.8% year-on-year, the highest since September, from 2.6% in March.
 On PCE: 
the PCE price index rose 0.4% in April (3.8% y/y), marking the third consecutive month of rapid inflation; the core PCE rose 0.2% (3.3% y/y).
 The March SEP projected 
a 30-bps increase in yearend 2026 PCE inflation (now 2.7%).
 
The softer-than-expected 0.24% m/m gain in core PCE in April buys the Fed a bit of time as it waits to see how higher oil prices will feed through to broader inflation pressures.
 Trajectory: **re-accelerating**, energy-led with secondary shelter stickiness.

**GROWTH**

Per the Atlanta Fed's GDPNow model, real GDP growth is currently tracking at 3% (quarter-over-quarter annualized basis) in Q2.
 However, 
Q1 GDP growth was revised down to 1.6% SAAR from 2.0% in the advance estimate, with the downward revision almost entirely due to a larger inventory drawdown.
 On labor: 
NFP is expected to rise ~85K in May (today's release), slowing from the 115K gain in April; the unemployment rate is forecast to hold at 4.3%.
 The three-month payroll run-rate is modest (~125–160K/month based on March's +178K and April's +115K). 
The third consecutive consensus-beating gain in nonfarm payrolls in May, if confirmed, should further reduce FOMC concern about downside labor market risks.
 On ISMs: 
Manufacturing PMI registered 54% in May, its highest reading since May 2022.
 
Among surveyed manufacturers, the Iran war was mentioned in 42% of comments and tariffs in 18%; 57% cited pricing volatility as an issue.
 Services: 
ISM Services PMI increased to 54.5 in May from 53.6 in April, the strongest gain in three months.
 
Employment contracted for a third consecutive month (47.9), while price pressures intensified to the highest since August 2022 (71.3) — diesel, gasoline, and oil again the primary culprits.


**CREDIT & LIQUIDITY**

As of early 2026, broad investment-grade OAS is roughly ~80 bps.
 HY OAS data from FRED is current but specific levels were unavailable in today's pull — directionally, 
the HY-IG spread is trending down and SPX is rising, indicative of near-term risk-on sentiment.
 DXY: 
the US Dollar Index (DXY) fell toward 99.18 before recovering to ~99.45 on Thursday, as investors digested Fed commentary ahead of today's NFP release.
 Financial conditions are loose overall — the curve is positively sloped, spreads are tight, and dollar weakness has provided an additional easing impulse. 
Treasury yields have found support from escalating Middle East tensions, which keep oil prices elevated and add to inflationary pressures.


**EARNINGS BACKDROP**

The S&P 500 climbed to another record high recently, closing at 7,580.
 
The index extended its advance for a ninth consecutive week, rising more than 5% in May.
 
Technology EPS estimates have been revised sharply higher since mid-February 2026,
 driven by AI infrastructure demand. 
The forward P/E on the S&P 500 stands at approximately 25.9x (Dec 2026 basis)
 — materially above the 10-year average of ~18–19x. 
The four largest hyperscalers — Amazon, Microsoft, Alphabet, and Meta — collectively spent north of $400 billion in capex in 2025, nearly 70% more than in 2024.
 The spread between AI capex intensity and near-term earnings delivery is a latent valuation risk.

**GEOPOLITICAL / EVENT TAPE**

A string of resilient US economic indicators has reinforced hawkish Fed expectations, while peace negotiations between the US and Iran are reportedly nearing their final stage — but Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said no meaningful progress had been made, and Iran-backed Hezbollah rejected a US-mediated ceasefire proposal, keeping geopolitical risks elevated.
 
Policymakers face an economic climate where inflation has held well above the Fed's 2% target, as tariffs and soaring energy prices complicate policy.
 
The next FOMC meeting is June 16–17, 2026.


---

## 🔴 PARAGRAPH 1 — REGIME CALL

**LATE-CYCLE.** The regime exhibits the classic late-cycle signature across all major pillars: 
positive but compressed spreads (2s10s +42 bps, 3m10y +76 bps) consistent with a cautious growth outlook and persistent inflation pressures that keep the Fed on hold.
 Nominal growth is still positive — GDPNow tracking +3% in Q2 — but Q1 was revised down to a soft 1.6%, labor is hiring at a decelerating pace, and 
services employment has now contracted for three consecutive months.
 Meanwhile, inflation is re-accelerating (CPI 3.8%, PCE 3.8% y/y) in a supply-shock context, leaving the Fed frozen on the sideline with a real policy rate barely above zero. This is textbook late-cycle: growth still above trend but losing momentum, inflation uncomfortably sticky/rising, policy rates on hold with a hawkish drift, term premium expanding, and equity markets priced for perfection at ~26x forward earnings.

---

## 🟡 PARAGRAPH 2 — WHAT'S WORKING / WHAT'S BREAKING

**What's Working:** Energy and defense are natural beneficiaries of the Iran war premium — 
S&P 500 forward EPS revisions are still trending higher even since the start of the Iran conflict, with energy estimates getting a boost.
 
Manufacturing at a 4-year high ISM (54%) with 19 straight months of broad expansion
 lifts Industrials and Materials. 
Technology estimates have been revised sharply higher,
 supported by the AI capex super-cycle; large-cap Tech/Semis (particularly AI infrastructure names like Oracle and Micron) are carrying the index to record highs. Quality/Profitability factors screen well in late cycle — companies with pricing power and low variable cost exposure to energy. Short-duration equities (Financials with steeper curve; Energy) and real assets outperform. **What's Breaking:** Rate-sensitive sectors are under pressure: 
inflation elevated, energy prices rising
 — this squeezes Consumer Discretionary margins and Housing. 
The prices-paid index at multi-year highs signals mounting supply-chain stress and margin pressure, likely restraining near-term investment and production for mid-cap Industrials.
 
Services employment is contracting for a third consecutive month
 — a leading stress indicator for labor-intensive sectors (Retail, Healthcare Services). Long-duration growth/SMID cap are most vulnerable to any further yield backup. The 26x forward P/E leaves essentially zero margin of safety for negative earnings surprises.

---

## 🟠 PARAGRAPH 3 — WHAT TO WATCH

**1. Today's May Nonfarm Payrolls (June 5) + June CPI (June 10):** 
Investors are awaiting today's nonfarm payrolls report for further clues on labor market strength
 — consensus expects ~85K but 
the third consecutive consensus-beating print
 would further close the door on any 2026 rate cut and 
markets are already pricing an 85% chance of a *hike* by year-end.
 June 10 CPI (May data) is equally pivotal: 
the Consumer Price Index for May 2026 is scheduled to be released on June 10 at 8:30 a.m.
 A third consecutive hot print would materially reprice the entire rates and equity complex. **2. June 16–17 FOMC Meeting + New Dot Plot:** 
The next FOMC meeting is June 16–17.
 With the April vote fracturing 8–4 and 
a majority of participants flagging that policy firming could become appropriate if inflation continues to run persistently above 2%,
 this meeting carries a live risk of a hawkish pivot in language or even a surprise hike — an event that would severely stress equities at current valuations. **3. Middle East Ceasefire/Escalation Binary:** 
Trump says peace talks are nearing their final stage, but Iran's foreign minister reports no meaningful progress, and Hezbollah has rejected a US-mediated Lebanon ceasefire,
 keeping the oil shock live. A genuine ceasefire would deliver a sharp disinflationary impulse — unlocking Fed cuts, compressing yields, and rotating the tape from Energy/Defense into rate-sensitive laggards. Escalation does the opposite and forces the Fed's hand higher. This is the single highest-optionality event on the 1–6 month horizon.
